frontmatter.title: Independent sums in categories of monomorphisms

mainmatter.p: “Independent sums” play an important role in the semantics of local state, as developed by Kammar, Levy, Moss, and Staton and further refined by Polzer and Goncharov. Although these authors did not elucidate the universal property of independent sums, they alude to Alex Simpson’s notion of independence structure — which ought to provide, after taking opposite categories, a suitable abstract interface to the notion of independent coproduct. We will explore a special case of this abstract structure that is more concrete than the general case, but nonetheless allows for a conceptual reconstruction of the ideas of Kammar et al. based on simple and intuitive universal properties.

A co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} } of monomorphisms in a category \mathcal{C} is called independent when its pullback is a span { \left \{ u \hookleftarrow \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{C} } \hookrightarrow v \right \} } in which \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{C} } is initial in \mathcal{C}.
Given a category \mathcal{C}, we define \mathbf{Inj} { \left ( \mathcal{C} \right )} to be the wide subcategory of \mathcal{C} spanned by monomorphisms.
The independent sum of a pair of objects u,v \in \mathcal{C} defined to be the initial independent co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} }, when it exists.
In an extensive category, any coproduct co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } is an independent sum.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: As soon as the co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } is independent, it evidently satisfies the universal property of independent sums, which is weaker than that of coproducts. Coproduct co-spans are always independent in an extensive category.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} be an adhesive category with a strict initial object, so that \mathcal{C} is also extensive. Then independent sums are created by the inclusion \mathbf{Inj} { \left ( \mathcal{C} \right )} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{C}.
mainmatter.block:
Proof.
Given a coproduct co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+ v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } in \mathcal{C} along with an independent co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow w \hookleftarrow v \right \} }, it suffices to check that the universal map u+ v \to w is a monomorphism. Equivalently, we wish to check that the following square is cartesian:
By the descent property for binary coproducts in an extensive category, it suffices to check that each of the following restrictions of that square along the coproduct injections is cartsian:
We will consider only the first square, which we rotate ninety degrees.
We see that the square above can be composed as follows so that the left-hand square is cocartesian and the outer square is cartesian.
Observing that all the vertical maps are monomoprhism, we use the “special pullback-pushout property” of Lack and Sobociński for adhesive categories to conclude that the right-hand component is cartesian. Thus it follows that u+v \to w is a monomorphism, and so we may define the independent coproduct { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u \oplus v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } in \mathbf{Inj} { \left ( \mathcal{C} \right )} by the coproduct co-span { \left \{ u \hookrightarrow u+v \hookleftarrow v \right \} } in \mathcal{C}.

frontmatter.title: Category-theoretic structure for independence and conditional independence
frontmatter.meta: The Thirty-third Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXXIII)

@article{simpson-2018, author = {Alex Simpson}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2018.03.028}, issn = {1571-0661}, journal = {Electronic Notes in Theoretical Computer Science}, note = {The Thirty-third Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXXIII)}, pages = {281-297}, title = {Category-theoretic Structure for Independence and Conditional Independence}, volume = {336}, year = {2018}, }

mainmatter.p: Relations of independence and conditional independence arise in a variety of contexts. Stochastic independence and conditional independence are fundamental relations in probability theory and statistics. Analogous non-stochastic relations arise in database theory; in the setting of nominal sets (a semantic framework for modelling data with names); and in the modelling of concepts such as region disjointness for heap memory. In this paper, we identify unifying category-theoretic structure that encompasses these different forms of independence and conditional independence. The proposed structure supports the expected reasoning principles for notions of independence and conditional independence. We further identify associated notions of independent and local independent product, in which (conditional) independence is represented via a (fibred) monoidal structure, which is present in many examples.

frontmatter.title: A monad for full ground reference cells
frontmatter.meta: Proceedings of the 32nd Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

@inproceedings{kammar-levy-moss-staton:2017, author = {Kammar, Ohad and Levy, Paul B. and Moss, Sean K. and Staton, Sam}, address = {Reykjavik, Iceland}, publisher = {IEEE Press}, booktitle = {Proceedings of the 32nd Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science}, year = {2017}, month = jun, doi = {10.1109/LICS.2017.8005109}, isbn = {978-1-5090-3018-7}, title = {A Monad for Full Ground Reference Cells}, }

frontmatter.title: Local local reasoning: a BI-hyperdoctrine for full ground store
frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{polzer-goncharov:2020, author = {Polzer, Miriam and Goncharov, Sergey}, editor = {Goubault-Larrecq, Jean and K\"{o}nig, Barbara}, address = {Cham}, publisher = {Springer International Publishing}, booktitle = {Foundations of Software Science and Computation Structures}, year = {2020}, isbn = {978-3-030-45231-5}, pages = {542--561}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2018.03.028}, title = {Local Local Reasoning: A BI-Hyperdoctrine for Full Ground Store}, }

mainmatter.p: Modelling and reasoning about dynamic memory allocation is one of the well-established strands of theoretical computer science, which is particularly well-known as a source of notorious challenges in semantics, reasoning, and proof theory. We capitalize on recent progress on categorical semantics of full ground store, in terms of a full ground store monad, to build a corresponding semantics of a higher order logic over the corresponding programs. Our main result is a construction of an (intuitionistic) BI-hyperdoctrine, which is arguably the semantic core of higher order logic over local store. Although we have made an extensive use of the existing generic tools, certain principled changes had to be made to enable the desired construction: while the original monad works over total heaps (to disable dangling pointers), our version involves partial heaps (heaplets) to enable compositional reasoning using separating conjunction. Another remarkable feature of our construction is that, in contrast to the existing generic approaches, our BI-algebra does not directly stem from an internal categorical partial commutative monoid.

frontmatter.title: Toposes are adhesive
frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{lack-sobocinski-2006, address = {Berlin, Heidelberg}, author = {Lack, Stephen and Soboci{\'{n}}ski, Pawe{\l}}, booktitle = {Graph Transformations}, editor = {Corradini, Andrea and Ehrig, Hartmut and Montanari, Ugo and Ribeiro, Leila and Rozenberg, Grzegorz}, isbn = {978-3-540-38872-2}, pages = {184--198}, publisher = {Springer Berlin Heidelberg}, title = {Toposes Are Adhesive}, year = {2006} }

mainmatter.p: Adhesive categories have recently been proposed as a categorical foundation for facets of the theory of graph transformation, and have also been used to study techniques from process algebra for reasoning about concurrency. Here we continue our study of adhesive categories by showing that toposes are adhesive. The proof relies on exploiting the relationship between adhesive categories, Brown and Janelidze’s work on generalised van Kampen theorems as well as Grothendieck’s theory of descent.
